skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Routes lip-sync requests to RunComfy models for existing videos, still portraits, or script-based generated speech using the runcomfy CLI.
skill.summary: Create lip-synced videos through RunComfy model routes and CLI commands.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The scanner findings are mostly expected for a skill that documents and invokes the runcomfy CLI. External commands, network calls, API token storage, and output file downloads are real operational risks, but no malicious intent, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration was found. Weak-cryptography and reconnaissance alerts appear to be false positives from model names, prose, and documentation tables.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Media Generation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill is designed to invoke `runcomfy run` through `Bash(runcomfy *)`. This is legitimate for the feature, but it executes an external CLI with user-selected media inputs and should remain restricted to the documented command scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and multiple documented invocations directly confirm CLI execution. The surrounding text limits usage to runcomfy and describes intended behavior, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Processing of User Media
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill sends video, image, and audio URLs to RunComfy model APIs and downloads generated outputs. This can expose user media to a third-party service and requires consent, endpoint allowlisting, and clear handling of sensitive assets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation names RunComfy endpoints and shows URL-based media inputs. It also states the CLI posts to the model API, polls status, and downloads output URLs.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Synthetic Media Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill enables pairing a face with a separate audio track. The file includes consent guidance, but the capability can be misused for impersonation, defamatory content, or non-consensual synthetic media if operator checks fail.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly describes the dual-use nature and consent requirements. The risk follows directly from the stated lip-sync functionality and does not depend on inferred code behavior.

low_findings.title: CLI Token Stored in User Configuration
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The documentation says `runcomfy login` stores an API token under the user configuration directory. It also states mode 0600 and offers an environment variable path for CI, so this is a managed credential-storage risk rather than credential theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden config path and token file are documented on one line. I found no evidence that the skill reads or exfiltrates that token itself.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines reference the skill description, model names, tips, and exit-code documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic functions, hashing algorithms, system enumeration, or reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is prose or documentation structure, not executable code. The scanner likely matched substrings such as model names, quality tips, or numeric exit codes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Restricted Bash Tool Executes runcomfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed tool scope is `Bash(runcomfy *)`, and examples invoke `runcomfy run`. Keep this command restriction in place and avoid adding broader shell access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command scope and invocation examples are explicit. The restriction reduces but does not eliminate external-command risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Media URLs Processed by Remote Service
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses URL inputs for source videos, portraits, and audio, then sends them to RunComfy model endpoints. This should be treated as third-party processing of user-supplied media.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show URL inputs, and the how-it-works section confirms model API submission. This is expected but security relevant.

content.value_statement: Creating lip-synced video requires choosing the right model for each media setup. This skill routes portrait, video, and script workflows to RunComfy commands with consent guidance.
actual_capabilities: Chooses a RunComfy lip-sync route based on source video, portrait image, audio, or script input.
Provides runcomfy CLI commands for Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, Creatify, Wan, and HappyHorse workflows.
Explains when to use premium, standard, avatar-style, and script-generated lip-sync models.
Documents required media URL fields for video, image, and audio inputs.
Includes consent, voice provenance, endpoint allowlist, and shell-scope guidance.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, authentication, and access to supported model endpoints.
Requires user-provided media URLs or scripts; it does not create local recordings by itself.
Quality depends on source footage, audio clarity, duration matching, and selected model tier.
Does not enforce consent automatically; operators must validate rights before use.

use_cases.title: Dub Existing Product Videos
use_cases.description: Route a source video and translated voiceover to Sync Labs for mouth motion that matches the new audio.

use_cases.title: Create Presenter Clips from Portraits
use_cases.description: Use OmniHuman with a portrait and approved voiceover to create a talking-head or avatar-style video.

use_cases.title: Prototype Script-Based Social Videos
use_cases.description: Select Kling or HappyHorse when a user has written copy but no recorded audio file yet.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Video Dub
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with existing video and audio URLs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Lip sync this source video to this approved voiceover. Use the best default route for an existing video and explain the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Portrait Avatar Clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with a portrait and audio track.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking avatar from this portrait and this consented audio file. Choose the RunComfy model route and note any framing or audio concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Model Comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that balances quality and cost.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the available RunComfy lip-sync routes for this project. Recommend premium, standard, and budget options with expected tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Language Batch Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced production planning for several localized versions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a multi-language lip-sync batch using one approved face asset and several approved audio tracks. Include routing, naming, and review steps.

output_examples.input: A brand video URL and a Spanish voiceover URL for an approved spokesperson.
output_examples.output: Recommended Sync Labs Pro for the existing video, listed the media fields needed, and described the expected output folder.

output_examples.input: A portrait image URL and a product pitch audio URL from the same consenting creator.
output_examples.output: Recommended OmniHuman, noted portrait framing requirements, and highlighted consent and voice provenance checks.

output_examples.input: A short social script with no recorded audio file.
output_examples.output: Recommended a script-based Kling or HappyHorse route and explained that audio will be generated rather than matched to a fixed recording.

best_practices: Confirm consent for both the face identity and the speaker voice before generating output.
Use clean speech audio without music beds when mouth accuracy matters.
Keep the RunComfy command scope restricted and send only user-approved media URLs.
anti_patterns: Do not target public figures or private people without clear permission.
Do not use broad shell access or remote install scripts for CLI setup.
Do not expect strong sync when audio length and source video duration differ greatly.

faq.question: Does this skill run local video models?
faq.answer: No. It routes requests through the RunComfy CLI and remote RunComfy model endpoints.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it lip-sync an existing video?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends Sync Labs or Kling routes when the user provides a source video and audio.

faq.question: Can it create a talking video from one portrait?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends OmniHuman when the user provides a portrait image and an approved audio track.

faq.question: Does it require consent checks?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill states that operators must refuse non-consensual or harmful synthetic media requests.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is sending user media to a third-party service through an external CLI.
